english: prop. pf. of śes-pa (shes pa), to know (a person or thing), to be acquainted, daṅ (dang) with Dzl.; ynyen-bśes (gnyen bshes) a relation, relative, ynyen-bśes-la phán-togs-par sems-so (gnyen bshes la phan thogs par sems so) they are intent on being of use to their relatives Dzl.; ynyen (gnyen) and bśes (bshes) may also be separated: kyód-la ynyen med bśes kyaṅ med (khyod la gnyen med bshes kyang med), Mil.; bśes-ynyén (bshes gnyen) on the other hand means: friend, dgé-bai bśes-ynyén (dge ba'i bshes gnyen) (Ssk. कल्याणमित्र) friend to virtue, spiritual adviser, opp. to mi-dgéi bśes-ynyén (mi dge'i bshes gnyen) seducer, Glr. (cf. sdíg-pai grógs-po (sdig pa'i grogs po)); dge-bśes (dge bshes) 1. = dgé-bai bśes-ynyén (dge ba'i bshes gnyen). 2. = dge-bsnyén (dge bsnyen) lay-brother. — ṅo bśes (ngo bshes) v. ṅo-śés (ngo shes). — mdza-bśés (mdza' bshes) friend.

pf. bsos (bsos), 1. also gsó-ba (gso ba), to pour out Lex. — 2. to lie with, to have sexual intercourse with, = _okrig-pa byéd-pa ('khrig pa byed pa), e.g.: de daṅ bsós-pas bu skyes (de dang bsos pas bu skyes) after having slept with him, she bore him a son Pth.; to engender, to generate, to beget (v.a.), phág-rdzis bsós-pai bu (phag rdzis bsos pa'i bu) the son begotten by the swine-herd Pth., (bsò-ba (bso ba) seems not to be considered obscene).
= bsàd-pa (bsad pa), *sò'-ri* (so ri) C. rumour, report, *sò-ri-la dhé-da zer dúg-te yi mi če* (so ri la the tshom zer dug te yid mi che) though it is rumoured I cannot believe it C.
C., W. liberal, munificent. Schr.: squanderer, spendthrift (?).
1. the letter s, the sharp English s, in C. distinguished from z (za), (which is sounded there also as sharp s) only by the following vowel being high-toned. — 2. num.: 28.

1. to put off, postpone, defer, delay, bód-la _ogró-ba (bod la 'gro ba) the going to Tibet Glr.; absolutely: bśól-ba bzaṅ (bshol ba bzang) it is good to wait; — to prolong, e.g. mi žig-gi _otsó-ba (mi zhig gi 'tsho ba) the life of a person (by a reprieve) Dzl., also to grant, to allow viz. a respite, a reprieve, bdág-la žag bdun žig bśól-te (bdag la zhag bdun zhig bshol te) granting me a respite of seven days Dzl.; to stop, detain, e.g. the sun in his course Thgy., a traveller wishing to set out Pth.; to omit, to neglect doing Mil. (ni f.); pyí bśól-ba (phyi bshol ba) to put off, postpone, pyí-bśól byéd-pai gáṅ-zag (phyi bshol byed pa'i gang zag) a person that is always postponing his religious duties Mil.; čós-la pyí-bśól byar mi ruṅ (chos la phyi bshol byar mi rung) Mil. there should be no putting off, whenever religion is concerned; bśol _odebs-pa (bshol 'debs pa) and _otebs-pa ('thebs pa) = bśól-ba (bshol ba) frq.; bśol ma tebs (bshol ma thebs) he could not be detained, kept back, diverted from his purpose Pth.; relatives are called bdúd-kyi bśol-_odébs (bdud kyi bshol 'debs) a hinderance on the way of the believer, caused by the devil.

resp. for zan (zan) or spags (spags), food, victuals, provisions of the table; bśos-la yśegs-pa (bshos la gshegs pa) to go to dine, to go to dinner Dzl.; dge-_odún-la bśos ysol-ba (dge 'dun la bshos gsol ba) to treat the priests to a meal Dzl.; now almost exclusively applied to food offered to the gods, = lha-bśos (lha bshos); bśos-bu (bshos bu) Mil. offering-morsels, e.g. small pieces of butter offered to the gods or the ghosts.

1. earth, as elementary substance, sa ču me rluṅ (sa chu me rlung) earth, water, fire, air, the four elements, sa nyúṅ-zad čig (sa nyung zad cig) a small quantity of earth, opp. to: sa čén-poi sa (sa chen po'i sa) the mass of the whole earth Dzl.; rdzá-sa (rdza sa) clay, argillaceous earth, *bé-sa* (be sa) W. sand and earth, śág-sa (shag sa) flint and earth; also for ore, metal (like rdo (rdo)), ysér-sa (gser sa) gold-ore, dṅúl-sa (dngul sa) silver-ore Cs.; kyím-sa (khyim sa) sweepings, offscourings;
